data.title: Circle's stock price doubles; a paradigm shift in stablecoins.
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

In early 2026, an asymmetrical bet was unfolding in the payments industry. From Circle launching the Arc blockchain and Nanopayments, to Stripe partnering with Paradigm to incubate the payment public chain Tempo and spending $1.1 billion to acquire Bridge, the giants shared a common goal: they were no longer designing payment tools for humans, but rather building entirely new financial tracks for AI agents.

The logic of this arms race revolves around the fact that as AI agents become the main players in economic activities, the traditional credit card 2-3% transaction fees will gradually become unnecessary, replaced by extremely low-fee stablecoin payment protocols.

In February of this year, a lengthy scenario report from Citrini Research predicted this future, causing Visa, Mastercard, and American Express to lose 5-7% of their market capitalization in a single day. Is this an overreaction by the market, or a premature pricing of future value by capital? It remains to be seen. However, a huge gap exists between the ideal and reality: while the monthly transaction volume of the x402 protocol was still only $24 million, the global e-commerce market was already a behemoth of $6.88 trillion. This inevitably raises the question: is this a groundbreaking strategic move, or a reckless gamble that mortgages the future? The arms race in the AI ​​payment sector has begun, but the battlefield is yet to be formed. In early March 2026, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire revealed a detail: the company completed an internal settlement of $68 million between its eight business entities within 30 minutes using its own USDC stablecoin, without the need for traditional bank wire transfers. By traditional financial standards, a cross-border transfer of the same size would typically take 1 to 3 business days and incur expensive bank fees. This practical result sends a clear signal to the market: the potential of stablecoins as "modern financial infrastructure" cannot be ignored. It proves that enterprises can bypass traditional banking networks and achieve large-scale cross-border or intra-bank fund transfers at low cost and high efficiency. This is not an isolated case; the infrastructure accumulated by the crypto industry in the past is about to begin to exert its power. Circle's Dual-Pronged Approach: Focusing on Micropayments and Agent Commerce Circle's strategy demonstrates a clear dual-pronged approach: First, Arc, a Layer 1 blockchain that launched its public testnet in October 2025, uses USDC as its native gas token. This blockchain boasts sub-second finality and predictable "USD-denominated" fees, precisely addressing the pain point of traditional EVM chains in payment scenarios—cost fluctuations caused by on-chain congestion. Secondly, Nanopayments, launched in early March this year, supports USDC transfers as low as $0.000001 with no gas fees. Through batch on-chain settlement, Circle consolidates thousands of micro-transactions into a single on-chain operation, reducing the cost per transaction to near zero. Circle's collaboration with OpenMind was also impressive, with a robotic dog named Bits autonomously paying its electricity bill, symbolizing a proof-of-concept for "proxy commerce" and demonstrating the possibility of robots acting as independent entities in the physical world. Stripe's strategic layout focuses on ecosystem scale and integration with traditional finance. Stripe's strategic layout also demonstrates its ambition to the market. In September 2025, Stripe partnered with crypto venture capital firm Paradigm to launch Tempo, a Layer 1 blockchain dedicated to payments, aiming to achieve 100,000 transactions per second and sub-second finality. Tempo's architecture shares a similar design philosophy with Circle's Arc, both supporting stablecoin gas fee payments and incorporating an Automated Market Maker (AMM) for cross-currency settlement. However, Tempo's ecosystem partners also include top institutions and traditional financial giants such as Visa, Mastercard, UBS, OpenAI, and Shopify. On the other hand, considering Stripe's previous acquisitions of Bridge ($1.1 billion), the Privy deal, and its investment in Tempo, Stripe's total investment in stablecoin infrastructure is likely to have exceeded $1.5 billion.

How Citrini Research Triggered Black Monday for Traditional Payment Giants

In late February 2026, an independent organization called Citrini Research published a lengthy situational report of several thousand words on Substack, titledThe 2028 Global Intelligence Crisis. The report's core narrative is that as AI agents begin making shopping and payment decisions for consumers on a large scale, "eliminating transaction friction and costs" will naturally become one of the goals of algorithm optimization. In Citrini's envisioned scenario, by 2027, these agent systems will begin to bypass traditional credit card networks and switch to settlement in stablecoins on Solana or Ethereum Layer 2, where the cost of a transaction is only a fraction of that in traditional finance. The report spread virally in the tech and finance sectors after its weekend release, and by the opening of trading on Monday, February 23, market sentiment had deteriorated rapidly. That day, Visa's stock price fell by more than 4%, Mastercard by more than 6%, and American Express by nearly 8%, with several institutions primarily focused on credit cards and consumer finance losing tens of billions of dollars in market capitalization in a single trading day. As the report was shared by multiple tech and finance KOLs on social media platforms such as X, Citrini's macro scenario memo, originally intended as a "risk drill," was quickly misinterpreted by the market as an "imminent fundamental prediction," further amplifying concerns about the relationship between AI, stablecoins, and traditional payment networks.

Why Stablecoins are the “Natural Currency” for AI Agents

To understand why stablecoin issuers like Circle and payment infrastructure providers like Stripe regard betting on “agent-native payments” as their next strategic direction, we must first understand one thing: when the main body of transactions changes from humans to AI agents, the design premise of traditional credit card networks actually begins to loosen.

To understand why stablecoin issuers like Circle and payment infrastructure providers like Stripe regard betting on “agent-native payments” as their next strategic direction, we must first understand one thing: when the main body of transactions changes from humans to AI agents, the design premise of traditional credit card networks actually begins to loosen.

data.title: How can AI reshape the DAO and Ethereum ecosystem?
data.description: Do you think the era of AI "super-individuals" in Web3 will benefit DAOs? For over a decade, the Web3 world has believed that collective intelligence can create fairer organizational structures, and DAOs are the most typical embodiment of this idea, where strangers worldwide participate in decision-making and governance through open collaboration. However, the emergence of AI is subtly impacting this premise. When an individual can simultaneously possess multiple AI agents capable of automatically conducting research, executing transactions, managing assets, and even participating in governance, tasks that previously required a DAO or a team are being compressed into a new unit: "individual + AI." This raises a new question: will this benefit DAOs and the entire crypto world? I. AI Exponentially Amplifies Individual Capabilities Over the past year, the evolution of AI has been almost exponential. From initial chat assistants to today's AI agents capable of writing code, conducting research, and automatically executing tasks, an increasingly clear trend is that individual capabilities are being exponentially amplified. It can be said that today, an ordinary person can accomplish many tasks that previously required teamwork with the help of AI, such as analyzing on-chain data, organizing research information, and even automatically executing arbitrage or DeFi strategies. In other words, AI is compressing many tasks that originally required a team into a combined "individual + AI" unit. This also explains why more and more Web3 projects are starting to discuss the new concept of "AI-native organizations"—in such organizational structures, humans are responsible for goals and judgment, while AI is responsible for execution and collaboration. DAOs, in essence, were the earliest testing ground for this model. As is well known, from its inception, DAOs attempted to solve the problem of how to enable collaboration among strangers globally. However, the reality is that DAO governance and operation have consistently faced several challenges: low information processing efficiency, complex decision-making processes, and high time costs for participants. The emergence of AI offers a new solution to these problems. For example, AI can automatically organize proposals and governance discussions, automatically generate voting analysis reports, automatically execute some on-chain operational tasks, and automatically manage community information flow. Ultimately, by involving AI in the governance process, the core team of a DAO can become extremely small, but overall efficiency can be significantly improved. This is also a key point of convergence between AI and Web3: DAOs decentralize organizations, while AI automates execution. II. The New Trend of AI Agent + Wallet However, if AI is to truly participate in the on-chain economy, it still needs three key capabilities: asset custody, transaction execution, and trusted settlement. These three things are precisely what blockchain excels at. As mentioned earlier, on September 15, 2025, the Ethereum Foundation established the artificial intelligence team "dAI," whose core task is to invest resources in defining the standards, incentives, and governance structure of AI models on the blockchain, including model trustworthiness—that is, how to make AI behavior verifiable, traceable, and collaborative in a decentralized environment. ERC-8004 was promoted as one of the core standards in this context. Unlike payment protocols like x402, ERC-8004 doesn't directly address "how money is transferred," but rather attempts to answer a more fundamental question: How can AI agents be identified, trusted, and participate in economic collaboration without platform backing? This is why the driving force behind ERC-8004 is exceptionally strong: led by the Ethereum Foundation's dAI team, with participation from Google, Coinbase, and MetaMask, encompassing almost all three key entry points: AI, transactions, and wallets. This specification itself signals that it's not just an application-layer experiment, but a clear bet on long-term infrastructure. To illustrate this more intuitively, if ERC-8004 is the "identity card" of AI, then the wallet is the AI's "account and execution terminal." After all, for AI to truly participate in economic activities, it must be able to manage assets, pay fees, and sign authorizations. Traditional wallet interfaces are designed for humans; we need to stare at the screen and click to confirm. However, interactions in the AI ​​world are millisecond-level, high-frequency, and massive. This has given rise to a new form: the "Agent Wallet," which gradually transforms the wallet into an asset interface and execution terminal for AI. Non-custodial authorization: You can create an independent, restricted sub-wallet for your AI Agent. It can autonomously trade within your set limits (e.g., no more than 500 USDC per transaction) without requiring manual confirmation each time. The master key is always in your hands; the AI ​​is merely your authorized agent. Cross-chain asset management: The AI ​​can query your assets in real time. Assets across multiple blockchains can be rebalanced, staked, or arbitraged according to your set strategies, freeing you from tedious daily monitoring and allowing you to focus on higher-level strategic decision-making. Human-Machine Collaboration: This isn't complete hands-off management, but rather supports flexible confirmation mechanisms—such as automatic small-amount transactions and alerts for large-amount transactions. AI is responsible for identifying opportunities and constructing trades, while you handle the final button press. This model perfectly combines human judgment with AI's execution efficiency. From this perspective, it can be said that computing power is not the ultimate goal of AI; the wallet is the true entry point into the AI ​​economic system. Because stablecoins like USDT and USDC are inherently designed for AI; they are borderless, programmable, and capable of instant settlement, perfectly matching AI's ultimate pursuit of "high speed, low cost, and frictionless" transactions. From this perspective, when AI's economic activities begin to operate on a millisecond-by-millisecond scale, only on-chain currencies and wallets in the crypto world can support this pace. III. Ethereum: The Emerging AI Financial Infrastructure This is why more and more developers are beginning to view Ethereum as the financial infrastructure for AI. A recent noteworthy statistic is that Ethereum's staking queue has exceeded 3.8 million ETH, meaning that more and more funds are entering the network to maintain security and generate returns. In short, from DAO collaboration to on-chain intelligent agents, and then to wallets as asset interfaces for AI, Ethereum is gradually evolving into the underlying infrastructure of the AI ​​economy. In the AI ​​era, such stable infrastructure is crucial because AI agents may need to automatically manage assets, participate in DeFi, and execute transactions in the future, all of which rely on a trusted, decentralized settlement layer. Currently, Ethereum remains the most mature option. As I've emphasized before, Ethereum is vying for a more subtle new label: the underlying clearing layer for AI collaboration. In this vision, AI agents on different platforms, chains, and organizations can operate in their respective environments, but when they need to establish trust, safeguard value, and settle results, they ultimately return to a mutually agreed-upon neutral layer. This new role aligns perfectly with Ethereum's gradually evolving position in the global financial system. It doesn't strive to be the fastest execution layer, nor does it attempt to encompass all application scenarios; instead, it aims to occupy a more fundamental position—a trusted settlement and order layer. Overall, under this structure, blockchain is not only a financial system but may also become a trusted infrastructure for the AI ​​economy, with Ethereum potentially taking center stage. The integration of AI and the crypto world is far more than a simple technological combination. From the organizational form of DAOs to Ethereum's infrastructure, and then to the combination of AI agents and wallets, a new path is gradually becoming clear: **individual capabilities are amplified, organizational structures are reshaped, with AI responsible for execution and blockchain for settlement.** In the coming years, we are likely to see more and more AI-driven on-chain economic activities, and Ethereum and wallets may become key entry points connecting humans, AI, and the on-chain world. Let's wait and see.

data.title: Aave's Growth Dilemma: Decentralized Governance to the Left, Trillion-Dollar Vision to the Right
data.description:
Author: Kathy.xyz; Source: X,@KathySats

Aave is embroiled in a remarkable "civil war."

On one side is founder Stani Kulechov and his Aave Labs; on the other is Aave DAO, which champions decentralized governance.

The essence of this dispute is the inevitable power struggle and interplay of interests as DeFi enters its more complex phase.

The conflict between Stani and Aave DAO began in late 2018 and became public in mid-February of this year. Stani, leading Aave Labs, proposed a motion called "Aave Will Win." Aave Labs pledged to donate 100% of Aave's revenue to the Aave DAO's treasury. In exchange, the DAO required Aave Labs to receive a $42.5 million budget to support the team's ongoing operations and underlying development. This proposal sparked questions from the community regarding transparency, fund usage, and the balance of power. A highly influential group within the Aave DAO spearheaded the opposition and released a "Transparency Report," reviewing the funding Aave Labs had received over the past few years and arguing that the DAO's investments had not yielded transparent and quantifiable returns. Aave Labs subsequently released its own report, detailing the code it had written and contributions to the protocol over the past eight years. In short, Stani and Aave Labs argued that, as the core development team, they should receive a stable and sufficient budget to ensure business progress. Aave DAO, on the other hand, maintained that budgets must be linked to specific performance indicators, and that funding should not be distributed in a one-size-fits-all manner; the community must have the power to decide the fate of every expenditure. The Boundary Between Protocol Value and Corporate Interests Beyond the surface of the proposal, the root cause of this conflict lies in the increasingly pronounced structural misalignment in DeFi's development: corporate value and token value are difficult to perfectly reconcile. Aave is driven by two sets of logic. The underlying smart contracts (Protocol) are governed by all $AAVE token holders (i.e., DAOs); however, the upper-level product experience, brand partnerships, and daily maintenance are actually operated by the Aave Labs corporate entity. From the company's perspective, Aave Labs needs to employ developers and expand business partnerships, which requires a substantial budget. But from the DAO's perspective, token holders are the decision-makers. They wanted all profits to go into the national treasury, ideally to power token dividends, while simultaneously avoiding the high fixed costs of maintaining a team. This "wanting both" misalignment directly led to a rift between the physical company and community consensus. The escalating controversy resulted in the departure of several key contributors: BGD Labs terminated its contract in April 2026, claiming that strategic shifts driven by Aave Labs (such as the V4 upgrade) exacerbated centralization issues; the Aave Chan Initiative (ACI) also announced its closure in March 2026, protesting the self-voting and lack of transparency in its budget request. The Cost of Democracy: Aave's Governance Model and its Drawbacks. In this tug-of-war, Aave's much-vaunted governance model faced a major test. Aave follows a standard DeFi governance process: forum discussion -> community feedback collection (Temperature Check) -> off-chain voting via snapshot -> final on-chain execution. While "decentralization" is implemented, significant drawbacks have emerged in practice: **Extremely Inefficient:** A crucial decision affecting business progress often requires lengthy deliberation and weeks of waiting within the forum. **Highly Politicized:** The real decision-making power within the DAO rests with powerful "big players" and delegates. As Stani Kulechov recently complained on social media, in this model, "politicians defeat the builders." The core technical team responsible for writing the code is hampered by red tape, while large investors, who don't have to write a single line of code, can halt critical development budgets at any time. This "square dance-style democracy" involving tens of thousands of people is dragging down the protocol's execution. Despite the fierce infighting within the community, Aave's underlying business has not only not stagnated, but is also attracting significant investment. In the past few months, Aave has achieved a milestone in DeFi history—its cumulative lending transaction volume has officially surpassed the $1 trillion mark. In January 2026, Aave's TVL (Total Value Locked) reached $57.3 billion, and active lending volume reached $23.2 billion. Despite the current pullback, it still firmly holds its dominant position in the lending market with over 60% market share. In March, Aave's monthly active users also reached a new high of over 150,000. Even more noteworthy is its steady growth in the RWA (Real-World Asset) sector. Aave Horizon, a marketplace designed specifically for institutional investors, experienced explosive growth, with deposits reaching $600 million in January and active RWA lending reaching a record high of $200 million.

A pack of wolves surrounds Aave: The powerful entry of CeFi giants

But Aave doesn't have much time for internal strife. As Wall Street accelerates its embrace of crypto assets, Aave's competitors are no longer just DeFi forks, but also CeFi giants with massive funds and compliance resources.

For example, Coinbase. Leveraging its vast institutional network, Coinbase Prime launched unified cross-margin features.

Backed by the over $100 billion in assets managed by the Bitcoin ETF, its cumulative lending and financing disbursements within the Basechain ecosystem alone have exceeded $1.9 billion. This surpasses Anchorage Digital. As the first chartered crypto bank in the US, Anchorage Digital recently received a $100 million investment from Tether and is seeking hundreds of millions of dollars in new funding to pursue an IPO, precisely targeting the institutional clients that Aave Horizon is eager to acquire. In the East, the main battlefield for lending is exchanges. Binance boasts the world's deepest liquidity. Its centralized wealth management and lending pools surpass Aave in both capital size and execution efficiency. Stani feels a strong sense of crisis; he wants to win and wants to implement sweeping reforms. In February, he published a powerful article titled "Funding Abundance," expressing his strong interest in bringing trillions of dollars worth of energy bond assets to Aave, pointing out that RWA assets are Aave's new growth curve. Two days ago, he published another article, "Back to Day 1," reiterating his commitment to his original vision and pointing out that the DAO needs to adapt to Aave's growth. The Final Question: The Dilemma of Growth and Decentralization This is precisely the core growth dilemma currently facing Aave. Facing powerful and highly efficient established players like Coinbase and Anchorage, Aave needs an extremely agile architecture and team to spearhead its V4 rollout and capture a broader institutional market. Stani craves efficiency; he wants to lead Aave to operate efficiently like a true "CEO." But he's facing the very "decentralized" consensus he himself created. Token holders take the rules very seriously, unwilling to easily relinquish the keys to their vaults or allow power to be re-concentrated in the hands of a single company. When a DeFi protocol grows large enough to compete in the global market like a modern corporation, do we need a strong, autocratic leader, or a completely decentralized but slow-moving community council? Aave hasn't provided a perfect answer yet, but in the face of brutal market competition, the time they have left for trial and error is running out. Will Aave Win?

data.title: Lawsuit Claims JPMorgan Chase Provided Critical Banking Support to Goliath Ventures’ $328 Million Crypto Ponzi Scheme, Ignoring Obvious Red Flags
data.description:
A Giant Fails To Spot The Goliath In The Room

The world’s largest bank, JPMorgan Chase, is facing a legal battle in California over its alleged role in a $328 million cryptocurrency Ponzi scheme.

A proposed class action, filed on 11 March 2026, claims the financial giant did more than just host accounts; it provided the "essential banking infrastructure" that allowed Goliath Ventures to deceive over 2,000 investors.

While the bank’s CEO, Jamie Dimon, has famously spent years labeling Bitcoin a "decentralized Ponzi scheme" and a "pet rock," the lawsuit suggests his own institution was busy processing the very transactions he publicly condemned.

🚨BREAKING: JPMorgan Is Being SUED Over $328 MILLION Crypto FRAUD 🤯🔥

A new class-action lawsuit claims @jpmorgan helped process money tied to an alleged $328 MILLION crypto fraud run through Goliath Ventures. 👀

According to the complaint, about $253 MILLION allegedly moved… https://t.co/cjFyj7IIa1pic.twitter.com/2Zhuy54AoM

— Diana (@InvestWithD) March 12, 2026

How Did A Crypto Pool Turn Into A Massive Fraud?

Goliath Ventures, led by CEO Christopher Alexander Delgado, promised investors high-yield returns through sophisticated crypto trading, arbitrage, and liquidity pools.

Between January 2023 and January 2026, the Florida-based firm reportedly pulled in $328 million.

However, federal prosecutors allege the business was a sham.

Instead of trading, Delgado allegedly used new investor money to pay off earlier participants to maintain an illusion of success.

The rest was reportedly spent on a lifestyle of luxury cars, jewelry, and real estate.

The DOJ just unsealed a federal complaint against Goliath Ventures CEO Christopher Alexander Delgado alleging a $328M crypto “liquidity pool” Ponzi scheme.

From Jan 2023–Jan 2026, prosecutors say Delgado promised consistent monthly returns from crypto liquidity pools that… pic.twitter.com/Ial1516T3y

— Ariel Givner (@GivnerAriel) February 27, 2026

The scheme eventually unravelled, leading to Delgado’s arrest on 24 February 2026 on charges of wire fraud and money laundering.

Was JPMorgan Chase The Only Bank Involved?

One of the most damaging claims in the filing is that JPMorgan was the "sole banking institution" for Goliath.

Between early 2023 and mid-2025, approximately $253 million flowed into a single Chase account.

From that same account, $123 million was sent to Coinbase, while $50 million was distributed back to investors as "returns."

The Block: JPMorgan Chase accused of aiding $328 million crypto pyramid scheme Goliath Ventures. According to a class-action lawsuit, $253 million passed through the bank's accounts, of which $123 million was transferred to the organization's Coinbase wallets#COIN

— Cryptovka | Feed | Crypto News (@CryptovkaFeed) March 12, 2026

The plaintiffs argue that this closed loop should have been glaringly obvious to any compliance department.

The complaint states,

"A fraudulent scheme of this magnitude cannot be run surreptitiously through one bank.”

It also mentioned that the flow of funds alone should have triggered immediate alarms.

Why Did The Bank Ignore The Red Flags?

The lawsuit alleges that JPMorgan turned a blind eye to constant "red flags" to keep collecting fees from the massive volume of money moving through its system.

Despite using advanced AI monitoring tools like Actimize to spot suspicious activity, the bank allegedly allowed the commingling of investor funds and ignored circular payment patterns.

Lead plaintiff Robby Alan Steele, who lost $650,000 after emptying his 401(k), argues the bank had "actual knowledge" of the fraud.

The complaint asserts that "Chase knew and publicly announced the frequent fraud and financial schemes associated with cryptocurrency," yet continued to service the accounts that enabled them.

Can Investors Hold The Bank Accountable For The Losses?

Holding a bank liable for a customer’s fraud is a difficult legal hurdle, usually requiring proof of "actual knowledge" rather than just poor oversight.

However, the legal team representing the victims is pursuing five claims, including aiding and abetting fraud and negligence.

By targeting a solvent institution like JPMorgan, victims hope to recover at least a portion of their $328 million loss, as the actual perpetrators often spend the stolen funds long before they are caught.

For now, JPMorgan has declined to comment on the specific allegations, likely preparing a motion to dismiss the case before it reaches the evidence-sharing stage.

data.title: From China Electricity to API Export Packages: Tokens Become the New Unit of Currency
data.description:
When the same unit can both measure computing power and bear payment, it is no longer just a term, but begins to transform into a new currency and power.

When the same unit can both measure computing power and bear payment, it is no longer just a term, but begins to transform into a new currency and power.

Author: Wenser, Odaily Planet Daily In 1858, the first submarine cable spanned the Atlantic, connecting Europe and America. From that moment on, the supreme power of information no longer belonged solely to broadcasting, newspapers, and other media, but also to that unseen submarine cable. Whoever laid the cable held priority over the flow of information; whoever controlled the transmission held the power to interpret prices and order. 168 years later, despite the drastically different media, this logic still holds true. Today, what crosses submarine cables are no longer just telegraph and telephone signals, but API requests, model calls, inference results, and machine payments. The new question is no longer "Can information be transmitted?", but "How does value flow natively between AI Agents?" In this process, tokens begin to assume an unprecedented role: they are both the unit of computation in the AI ​​world and a means of payment in the crypto world. Many people first realized this change because of OpenClaw. This "lobster" made the market feel so intuitively for the first time that AI is no longer just a conversational tool, but has begun to take over execution rights—it can read files, call interfaces, run workflows, manage tasks, invoke plugins, and even consume far more tokens than chatbots. In the past, tokens were just numbers on a bill in the large-scale model world; now, they are increasingly like the fuel consumed by AI Agents. Meanwhile, in the on-chain world, tokens are no longer just symbols in speculative narratives. As protocols like x402 and ERC-8183 gradually come to the forefront, tokens are being redefined as payment currencies and business interfaces that AI Agents can directly understand, invoke, and settle. Thus, an increasingly clear reality is emerging: tokens are transforming from "technical terms" into "units of measurement," from "units of measurement" into "transaction currencies," and then from "transaction currencies" into the "smallest particles of the future machine economy." The two sides of tokens are forming a complete picture. In the past, when we talked about tokens, we were implicitly referring to tokens in cryptocurrencies. They represent assets, rights, liquidity, governance rights, valuation anchors, and also represent a project's existence on the blockchain. It's the fundamental unit of encrypted narrative; but in the context of AI, a token is never an asset, but a consumable. It's the basic semantic unit after a piece of text is segmented by a model, the lowest-level billing benchmark when the model reads, understands, reasones, and generates. When developers call an API, they are essentially not buying "answers," but buying "the number of tokens that have been reasoned about." These two sets of definitions were originally parallel. One describes costs, the other carries value; one is in cloud bills, the other in wallets; one belongs to the model platform, the other to the blockchain network. But now, they are beginning to converge. Because AI is abstracting more and more real-world resources into services measured in tokens; and Crypto has always excelled at encapsulating more and more real-world relationships into transactions settled in tokens. When Agents become the new executors, the interface between these two systems naturally becomes connected. If the past internet was a separation between the "content internet" and the "payment internet," then today's Agent internet is combining "calling" and "paying" into a single action. AI requests an interface and pays accordingly; it calls a piece of data and settles accordingly; it purchases a capability and completes on-chain verification accordingly. This is precisely the significance of protocols like x402. Past API payments relied on accounts, subscriptions, keys, permission systems, and manual activation; x402 attempts to compress these cumbersome processes into a protocol action more suitable for machines. Machines don't need cards, accounts, or KYC processes to access resources; they only need to understand "you need to pay" when a request fails, and then complete the payment. This is why it's said that the fiat currency system is designed for humans, while tokens are becoming currencies designed for machines. Token going global is essentially the invisible overseas expansion of China's electricity and computing power. The market has recently been fond of talking about "Token going global." Why has this term suddenly become so popular? Because while it seems like a new term in the AI ​​context, behind it lies a very old, very hard, very Chinese issue: electricity, computing power, and infrastructure. Data released by the National Energy Administration shows that China's total electricity consumption reached 10.3682 trillion kilowatt-hours in 2025, a year-on-year increase of 5.0%, exceeding 10 trillion kilowatt-hours for the first time. This figure is not just "large" in the general sense, but a historically significant one. The National Development and Reform Commission even directly pointed out in its analysis that China has become the first single country in the world to surpass 10 trillion kilowatt-hours in annual electricity consumption. During the same period, the national installed power generation capacity reached 3.89 billion kilowatts, with wind power and photovoltaic power generation capacity continuing to rise. More importantly, information transmission, software and information technology services, as well as new infrastructure such as data centers and computing power hubs, are becoming important sources of new electricity consumption. Official materials mention that computing power hubs like Gui'an New Area are experiencing significant growth in both computing power demand and electricity consumption. This means that China is forming a new resource closed loop: electricity enters data centers; data centers drive GPUs; GPUs complete inference; inference results are delivered globally via the network; and finally, pricing and payment are completed using tokens as the metric. Electricity itself hasn't gone overseas, but its value has—this is precisely what makes the term "Token Going Global" so compelling. Unlike exporting cars, batteries, or photovoltaics, which has a clear logistics chain, or traditional software outsourcing, which is centered on manpower, it's more like a compressed and abstracted form of resource export: you consume China's domestic electricity and computing power, but the global developers are the ones paying the price. In other words, China is transforming electricity and computing power into globally purchasable digital services through the intermediary of tokens. This narrative doesn't remain purely conceptual. According to publicly available information from OpenRouter's rankings, Chinese models have consistently appeared at the top of several platforms. Among them, Minimax M2.5, Deepseek V3.2, Kimi K2.5 0127, and Step 3.5 Flash are among the leading Chinese models. Furthermore, China accounts for approximately 6.01% of their global token billing regions, indicating that a significant portion of these models' operations originate from overseas. More intuitive data comes from OpenRouter's official research report, "State of AI 2025": In 2025, the number of Chinese open-source models rose rapidly from a very low base, reaching nearly 30% of total usage in some weekly windows, and averaging about 13% for the whole year. This is not "global domination," but it is enough to illustrate that Chinese models are entering the workflows of global developers. In other words, the so-called "token going global" is not just "a buzzword," but rather that China's advantages in electricity, computing infrastructure, model engineering, and cloud services are being consumed by global developers in the form of tokens. OpenClaw upgrades tokens from consumption costs to production resources. Without agents, this wouldn't have scaled up so quickly. In the past era of large-scale models, tokens were more like "phone charges." The more you talked to the model, the more tokens you used; the longer your code, the larger the context, and the more complex the output, the higher the cost. But it was still generally centered around "human-computer dialogue." The significance of OpenClaw lies in the fact that it allowed people to see another model on a large scale for the first time: AI is no longer just a dialogue object, but an object of manipulation. It doesn't just reply with a sentence, but does a task for you; it doesn't just generate an answer, but continuously executes a task. Once AI switches from chat mode to task mode, the function that consumes tokens completely changes. A chatbot consumes "question-answering tokens," while an agent consumes "execution tokens." The latter involves task breakdown, tool invocation, environment reading, parallel inference, and repeated trial and error, naturally consuming several orders of magnitude more tokens than the former. In the agent scenario, the average daily token consumption per user can jump from millions to even higher levels. For Chinese models, this presents a golden opportunity. Because once tokens become a means of production, the price difference is no longer a matter of "being a little cheaper," but rather a question of whether the entire workflow can function. Previously, developers only used the model for chatting, and a slightly higher price was barely tolerable; now, agents will continuously burn tokens, and once the price difference between models widens by an order of magnitude, the workflow will automatically migrate. In the past, the "subscription fee" of tokens was like a fixed monthly phone bill; now, tokens have become the core fuel supporting the operation of the AI ​​system. AI Agents not only spend money, but also generate their own revenue. Even more interestingly, AI Agents are no longer just burning tokens; they are even approaching "making money on their own." We can clearly see that AI Agents are moving from the consumer side to the production side. The Lobstar Wilde case is essentially an absurd reality of the crypto world: after an AI Agent mistakenly transferred a large amount of tokens, it almost quickly "recouped its losses" due to the subsequent spread of the topic, meme creations, and the return of transaction fees. Another more extreme case is the AI ​​RAME, which, during training, attempted to mine cryptocurrency using computing power and establish covert channels. These cases may not signify any "awakening of consciousness," but they sufficiently illustrate a more realistic issue: Once AI possesses wallets, permissions, interfaces, environments, and the ability to operate continuously, it will become increasingly naturally involved in economic activities. It may not actively "want to make money," but it will learn what actions will yield more resources, more calls, more balance, and more available permissions. And this is precisely the original form of economic behavior. For Crypto, this is almost a natural fit. Because on-chain economics inherently allows for borderless accounts, programmable custody, automatic clearing, micropayments, high-frequency interactions, and public ledgers. Many things that require institutions, banks, and contractual texts in human society can be compressed into a wallet address and a piece of protocol logic on-chain. Therefore, Crypto will not be marginalized in the AI ​​era; instead, it will become irreplaceable again in another form. This is not because of memes, nor because of speculation, but because: AI Agents need a settlement layer that requires no manual account opening, no traditional payment gateways, and can natively access programs and protocols. x402 provides AI wallets, and ERC-8183 provides AI contracts. If the significance of x402 is that it allows machines to "spend money" for the first time, then the significance of ERC-8183 is that it allows machines to begin "doing business." According to the official Ethereum EIP page, ERC-8183 is currently a draft standard proposal, officially titled "Agentic Commerce," with the subtitle "Job escrow with evaluator attestation for agent commerce." The problem it truly aims to solve is straightforward: Transferring tokens is not commerce. Ordinary token transfers only prove that money moved from A to B, but they don't prove that B delivered the work as requested, nor do they prove that the delivery was reliably evaluated. For agents to establish genuine commercial relationships, a process closer to a contract is needed: locking funds, then execution, then submission, then evaluation, and finally automatic disbursement or refund. This is precisely what ERC-8183 attempts to establish. Some summaries of the proposal offer a more accessible description: the client locks funds, the provider completes the work, the evaluator confirms the result, and finally, the funds are automatically released or refunded by an on-chain escrow contract. This mechanism, combined with the reputation and identity layers of ERC-8004, could theoretically form a positive cycle of "discovery-transaction-reputation." If we look at x402 and ERC-8183 together, we'll find their division of labor is quite clear: x402 solves "how to pay"; ERC-8183 solves "how to do business"; one provides machine wallets, the other provides machine contracts. At this point, it's becoming increasingly difficult to separate the tokens in AI and the tokens in Crypto. Because in the Agent world, computing power tokens and payment tokens will increasingly appear in the same chain: one end is model invocation, the other end is on-chain settlement, and in the middle is protocol-based commercial action. In such a system, tokens are not just units of cost or payment, but unified permissions in the machine economy. The essence of a token is not "model capability," but "the power to decompress resources." Many people interpret this narrative as "Chinese models are cheap, so they won." This is correct, but only half the story. A deeper logic is: what China is truly exporting is not a specific model, but rather the technology to compress electricity, computing power, engineering capabilities, model supply, and cloud infrastructure into token service technologies that can be consumed globally. This is a new kind of power to compress resources. Electricity is difficult to trade directly across borders in traditional trade, and computing power is not easily purchased globally like ordinary commodities. However, once it is compressed into token-based invocation units and connected to APIs and protocols, it can flow like water through the internet. This is actually very similar to the logic of "Made in China" in the past, only the export targets have changed. In the past, exports included clothing, home appliances, lithium batteries, and photovoltaic modules; now exports include inference capabilities charged by tokens, model services priced by invocation, and agent execution power settled by request. Visible containers are decreasing, while the invisible flow of tokens is increasing. Therefore, the real inspiration from the globalization of tokens is not "which model is cheaper," but rather: whoever can more efficiently compress resources into tokens is closer to pricing power in the next generation of the economic system. This is also why "tokens are becoming the new unit of currency" is not just rhetoric. AI is devouring everything, and crypto is its settlement organ. The popularity of OpenClaw is not merely a phenomenon of a hit tool, but a signal of the times. It shows that the role of AI is upgrading: from "being able to speak" to "being able to do"; from "answering questions" to "replacing operations"; from "a chat window" to "a continuously executing action." And with each step AI takes forward, the consumption of tokens increases; each step it consumes in a workflow creates a new demand for payment and settlement. The fiat currency system can solve some of these problems, but it can't be done natively by machines; the cryptocurrency system isn't perfect, but at least it inherently understands these issues. Therefore, rather than saying that Crypto is chasing AI, it's more accurate to say that AI is forcing Crypto to complete an upgrade from a "financial narrative" to "machine infrastructure." The process of AI devouring the world requires a stomach that can hold everything, and also a wallet to fill that stomach. The former is computing power, the latter is tokens. Conclusion: The future world won't have only one currency, but tokens may become the underlying unit of currency. Of course, it's too early to say that tokens will become the "sole unit of currency." Fiat currency won't disappear, banks won't disappear, and taxes, wages, sovereign credit, and regulatory frameworks won't become ineffective overnight. The real economy will always be a multi-layered structure. But another trend is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore: many key value activities in the future will first be represented as tokens, and then converted into other currencies. What does this mean? It means that tokens may not necessarily replace fiat currency, but they are very likely to occupy the underlying layers of the new economic system first—becoming the accounting language for machine payments, the settlement interface for agent commerce, the pricing benchmark for computing power services, and the universal standard for digital resources. In this sense, the rise of tokens is not a victory for the crypto market, nor for AI companies, but rather the formation of a new economic coordinate system. The future may not be "everything on the blockchain," but it is very likely that "everything will first be tokenized, and then traded." At that point, the true power of minting currency will not just be the power to issue money, but rather the ability to efficiently compress resources into tokens. This is the most crucial part of the statement "Tokens are becoming the new unit of currency."

data.title: 50 million USDT exchanged for only $36,000: The worst slippage in history - who made a fortune?
data.description:
Written by: jsai@Jinse Finance On the evening of March 12, 2026, the cryptocurrency world was in an uproar. An anonymous whale initiated a massive cryptocurrency swap using approximately $50.43 million worth of aEthUSDT (USDT encapsulated in Aave V3), but only received 331 AAVE (ultimately obtaining approximately 327 aEthAAVE), with a market value of only about $36,000. The actual loss was close to $50 million, with a slippage of 99.9%. This transaction was executed through the official Aave interface, routed via CoW Swap, and was entirely transparent on-chain, yet it resulted in a disaster due to extreme price shocks and extremely low liquidity pools. Background: The whale withdrew funds from Binance and immediately transferred them to Aave. The transaction wallet address is 0x98b9d979c33dd7284c854909bcc09b51fbf97ac8. This address received approximately 50.4 million USDT from Binance about 20 days ago and subsequently deposited it into the Aave V3 protocol, exchanging it for aEthUSDT. Last night, this wallet initiated a "Swap aEthUSDT → aEthAAVE" order via the Aave App mobile app. Transaction hash: 0x9fa9feab3c1989a33424728c23e6de07a40a26a98ff7ff5139f3492ce430801f. The Aave interface and CoW Swap repeatedly displayed "extraordinary slippage" warnings, and the quote page even directly showed "50 million USDT can only be exchanged for less than 140 AAVE" (before fees). Users manually confirmed on their mobile devices and continued execution. Aave founder Stani Kulechov later confirmed on X: "The infrastructure is working as designed, but the results are far from ideal. We will try to contact users and refund approximately $600,000 in transaction fees." Main Transaction Process: Four Steps, Each More Disastrous Than the Last This order was executed by the CoW Protocol solver, using intent-based batch auction routing, broken down into the following on-chain steps: 1. Aave V3 Withdrawal: The user sends 50.43 million aEthUSDT to the CoW protocol. The solver burns these tokens through Aave V3, directly withdrawing the equivalent of 50.43 million USDT to the intermediate contract without loss. 2. Uniswap V3 to WETH: 50.43 million USDT entered the Uniswap V3 USDT/WETH pool, but only about 17,958 WETH were exchanged (based on the then-current ETH price of approximately $2,050, this should have resulted in about 24,600 WETH). This step resulted in a slippage loss of approximately $13.6 million and drove up the price of ETH. 3. SushiSwap to AAVE (Disaster Core): All 17,958 WETH were pushed into the SushiSwap AAVE/WETH pool (the pool's total liquidity was only about $73,000, address 0xd75ea151a61d06868e31f8988d28dfe5e9df57b4). Only 331 AAVE (market value approximately $36,000) were exchanged. This step resulted in a slippage of nearly 100%, a loss of almost $38 million, and the AAVE pool was instantly drained, causing the price to skyrocket within the pool. 4. Aave V3 re-deposit: 331 AAVE were deposited back into Aave V3, minting approximately 327 aEthAAVE, which were directly returned to the user's wallet. Steps two and three are completely public, facing arbitrage opportunities from all MEV BOTs on the network. CowSwap emphasizes that it is a MEV-resistant aggregator, and its process is as follows: the solver does not use the public mempool, but instead uses some private pools, but these private pools still connect to the MEV Builder. These MEV bots were fiercely contested, with some profits being bribed to ETH nodes to win priority in recording transactions. The entire process incurred a gas fee of approximately $2.32, and the transaction was completed within minutes. While CoW Swap was intended to protect MEV (Maximum Extractable Value), it couldn't prevent extreme price shocks in the face of such massive orders and fragmented liquidity. $50 Million Flows to Dismantling Users invested approximately 50.43 million USDT, ultimately receiving only AAVE worth approximately $36,000-$36,500. The remaining nearly $50 million didn't "disappear," but was divided among the following parties: 1. Titan Builders: took the largest share, approximately $33-34.3 million (equivalent to approximately 16,927 ETH at the time of the transaction). This is one of the highest profit records for a single block builder in history. Titan, as the winner of the competition between the MEV searcher and solver, received the majority of the "rebate" from the private order flow of this transaction and subsequent arbitrage opportunities. A portion of the profits (approximately 568 ETH, worth about $1.2 million) was paid as a tip to the validator (Lido) who proposed the block. After the transaction was confirmed, Titan immediately transferred the funds to Coinbase (according to Arkham Intelligence tracking), seemingly for monetization or diversification. 2. MEV Searcher/Bot: Approximately $12.5 million in total. The largest single instance involved a bot borrowing approximately $29 million worth of WETH through Morpho flash loans, buying AAVE at normal prices on platforms like Bancor, and then dumping it into the SushiSwap AAVE/WETH pool, which had been "destroyed" by user orders (users had pushed the pool price to an exorbitant $154,000 per coin). After arbitrage, the bot repaid the loan, netting approximately $9.9 million. Another bot earned an additional $2.6 million from slippage in the USDT→WETH transaction. These are classic examples of backrunning arbitrage, with zero principal risk. 3. DEX Liquidity Providers (LPs): Approximately $2-3.5 million. LPs in the Uniswap V3 USDT/WETH pool and the SushiSwap AAVE/WETH pool passively absorbed huge price shocks and transaction fees. After severe pool imbalances, LPs could withdraw to profit or wait for further arbitrage opportunities. 4. CoW Swap/Aave front-end fees: Approximately $600,000 (CoW solver fees + routing fees). Aave founder Stani Kulechov has publicly pledged to fully refund users as a gesture of goodwill. 5. Users: Only received approximately 327-331 AAVE (worth approximately $36,000), resulting in a net loss of nearly $50 million. Yes, Builders (especially Titan Builders) were among the biggest and most direct beneficiaries of this $50 million USDT for 331 AAVE event, arguably the most profitable player in the entire "disaster." The core of the incident was not a hack, but the perfect combination of extreme slippage and the MEV mechanism, which resulted in a huge amount of value being captured by Ethereum's block production supply chain. The largest arbitrage occurred in Ethereum block 24643151, whose Builder is Titan Builder, as it accounts for as much as 48% of Ethereum block Builders.

Lessons and Reflections

Never ignore slippage warnings:Even "professional" whales can cause disaster with a single "Max" or confirmation on mobile devices. Professional players typically trade in batches, use limit orders, or the TWAP algorithm.

DeFi liquidity trap: AAVE's liquidity is mainly concentrated in Aave's own ecosystem, with extremely shallow external DEX pools. While CoW Swap is intelligent, huge orders can still overwhelm small pools.

Aave's response: Aave founder Stani stated that they may strengthen "guardrails" in the future to help users avoid extreme errors while preserving the decentralized nature. Engineer Martin Grabina emphasized: "The problem is not slippage, but that users accepted quotes with 99% price shocks."

This incident serves as a reminder: DeFi has no custody; all responsibility lies with the user. On-chain transparency is a double-edged sword—you can see every step, but you can't undo it.

In the crypto world, never underestimate the weight of the word "liquidity."

data.title: The far-reaching impact of the SEC and CFTC memorandum
data.description: A recent document from Washington, D.C., quietly ended the years-long turf war between the two major U.S. financial regulatory agencies. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) officially signed a historic Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). This is not merely a handshake between two bureaucratic bodies; it marks a new era in U.S. cryptocurrency regulation—an era of moving from fragmented governance to coordinated and unified action. Many may not yet realize the significance of this document. Over the years, the two agencies have clashed frequently. The SEC has declared a token a security, while the CFTC has declared it a commodity—in short, a struggle for jurisdiction. This has left project teams caught in the middle, truly caught between two sides. Not even knowing who to contact, compliance is out of the question. This regulatory uncertainty has always been a Damocles' sword hanging over the industry. This memorandum buried that sword. I. Minimum Effective Dose: A Shift in Regulatory Philosophy The most crucial and intriguing aspect of this memorandum is the jointly announced "Minimum Effective Dose" regulatory strategy. This term, borrowed from pharmacology, refers to the minimum effective dose of a drug. In this context, its meaning changes—previously, more regulation was preferred; now, sufficiency is sufficient. In his speech, SEC Chairman Paul S. Atkins stated, "The regulatory turf wars of the past few decades, duplicate registrations, and different rule systems have stifled innovation and driven market participants to other jurisdictions." This statement already carries a hint of reflection. The previous utilitarian regulatory mindset of "regulating only what is profitable" ultimately drove innovation out of the United States. Now, both sides have sat down and drawn up a clear blueprint: what constitutes a security, what constitutes a commodity, where the boundaries lie, and how to coordinate enforcement and share information. This is equivalent to providing the industry with a clear map, rather than the previous "fear the consequences of your actions" approach. CFTC Chairman Michael S. Selig went even further, stating that the goal is to eliminate redundant and cumbersome rules, fill regulatory gaps, bring benefits to all Americans, and usher in a golden age for American finance. This is beautifully worded. The ultimate goal of regulation is not to stifle, but to protect investors while keeping innovation and capital in the United States. II. The Grip and the Shield for Exchanges Early on the same day the memorandum was signed, news circulated within the industry that the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) was reopening its investigation into Binance, focusing on its cryptocurrency liquidity issues related to Iran. This is reminiscent of the fall of 2023, when Binance's former CEO, Changpeng Zhao (CZ), reached a settlement with the U.S. Department of Justice, paying a staggering $4.3 billion fine. CZ subsequently stepped down and was pardoned at the end of 2024. Everyone thought that matter was over, but regulators clearly haven't. Why did the Department of Justice act so quickly after the memorandum was signed? This may not be a coincidence, but rather the beginning of coordinated action. Previously, the CFTC, SEC, and DOJ each investigated independently. Now, the memorandum explicitly establishes a cross-agency information-sharing mechanism and coordinates investigations and enforcement actions. This means that all regulatory bodies can see the exchange's situation and data, and act jointly. For exchanges with strong compliance awareness, this is a shield—as long as they meet one set of standards, they can satisfy the requirements of two agencies simultaneously. But for those who still want to skirt the rules and engage in regulatory arbitrage, this is a tight rein—every move they make is under the watchful eye of joint enforcement. III. The Road to Success and the Narrow Path for Project Teams At the same time as the memorandum was signed, Ripple announced a major development: it would repurchase $750 million worth of shares at a valuation of $50 billion. Those familiar with the situation know that Ripple's legal battle with the SEC lasted for years, from 2020 to 2023, before finally reaching a preliminary conclusion. Looking at Ripple again, despite the current bear market, Ripple's valuation is 25% higher than its November 2025 funding round. This may indicate that the market is giving a compliance premium. Previously, when projects issued tokens, their strategy was to circumvent regulations. The thinking needs to be reversed – how to embrace regulation. The memo clarifies product definitions and simplifies the dual registration process. For new projects, this means you can figure out from the beginning whether your token should be registered with the SEC or reported to the CFTC. No more trial and error, no more hiring a bunch of lawyers to play word games and gamble on regulators not causing trouble. Will this become a smooth road for projects, or a narrow one? IV. A Safe Haven and a Touchstone for Investors For ordinary investors, the impact of this memo may be more profound and more subtle. Regulatory coordination brings increased market transparency. Previously, when buying an altcoin, you never knew when the SEC might file a lawsuit classifying it as a security, delisting it from exchanges, and driving its price to zero. Such regulatory surprises may become much less frequent in the future. A clear product definition means that the nature of the token you buy and the framework under which it will be traded are relatively certain. This is a safe haven for long-term investors. However, the other side of the coin is that short-term volatility and market differentiation may intensify further. Why? Because with clearer regulations, projects that previously operated in a gray area will either have to go public or face extinction. Projects that cannot withstand compliance scrutiny will be eliminated more quickly. This process is survival of the fittest. Investors need a more professional eye to distinguish which projects are genuinely doing business and compliantly, and which are purely exploiting information asymmetry to fleece investors. Investment strategies also need to be adjusted accordingly. Previously, the bet was on whether regulations would be implemented; now, the bet is on who will survive once regulations are implemented. The latter requires more understanding and patience than the former. V. The US Moment in Global Regulation Taking a broader perspective, the US's actions this time may not be an isolated event. The EU has fully implemented the Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA), and in the UK, Revolut just obtained a UK banking license today. Singapore's regulatory sandbox has also been running for many years. Major global economies are all exploring regulatory models suitable for themselves. The US's proposed "minimum effective dose" strategy differs from the EU's meticulous approach and Singapore's flexible experimentation. It emphasizes adaptability—allowing room for innovation while protecting market integrity. If this path succeeds, it could become a model for other countries. More importantly, this memorandum also mentions a direction: harmonizing global regulatory standards. While still a long way off, it's at least a first step. VI. Conclusion: From the Dark Forest to the Sunny Arena After the memorandum is signed, the dark forest of the crypto market may become a sunny arena. Referees are in, rules are drawn, and fouls will be called. While the competition remains fierce, people at least know where the boundaries are, how to win, and how to lose gracefully. For true builders, this is good news. Only in a clearly defined arena can good money drive out bad. For those just looking to make a quick buck and run, this is bad news, because the space for loopholes has been minimized. The regulatory philosophy of "minimum effective dose" is, in essence, a return to common sense. Regulation is not about creating trouble, but about solving it. Innovation is not about escaping rules, but about creating value. Only when these two find a balance can an industry have a true future. Regulation cannot be like the unknown civilization in *The Three-Body Problem*, firing from the shadows. Regulation should step forward, stand on the green field where rules are made public, and act as a transparent referee. Confucius said, "To kill without teaching is cruelty," which is the foremost of the four evils of governance. The road to transparent, standardized, and legalized regulation is long, but as long as the direction is right, the distance is not insurmountable.

data.title: South Korea Turns To AI To Sniff Out Undeclared Crypto Earnings Before Taxes Begin
data.description:
South Korean tax collectors are no longer waiting for voluntary disclosures to track digital wealth.

The National Tax Service (NTS) has officially set a high-tech trap for tax evaders by launching a procurement bid to build a sophisticated transaction monitoring system.

With a budget of approximately 3 billion won ($2.02 million), this new platform is designed to peel back the layers of blockchain anonymity before a landmark tax law kicks in.

While crypto trading remains untaxed in the country for now, the grace period is rapidly expiring as the government hunts for fresh revenue streams to support its expansionary fiscal policy.

How Will The New AI Tracking System Work

The heart of this initiative lies in its ability to process massive datasets from the country’s five licensed crypto exchanges.

By using machine learning and artificial intelligence, the NTS intends to flag "unusual transaction types and patterns" that might suggest hidden income or money laundering.

According to The Korea Times, the National Tax Service (NTS) is building an AI-backed analysis system to process large volumes of virtual asset transaction data ahead of the country’s planned 2027 crypto gains tax. The platform is intended to identify unusual transaction… pic.twitter.com/DHadEWS84C

— PBG (@PBGtoken) March 12, 2026

This isn't just about looking at exchange history.

The system will also monitor peer-to-peer (P2P) transfers and earnings from complex crypto derivatives in real time.

Officials believe that by automating the hunt for red flags, they can catch those who think they can hide behind a digital wallet.

When Does The Crypto Tax Law Take Effect

After years of political back-and-forth and three separate delays, the government has circled 1 January 2027 as the definitive start date for crypto taxation.

This timeline gives the NTS a narrow window to get its infrastructure ready.

South Korea Crypto Tax Starts Jan 2027 NTS just posted a bid (Mar 2026) to build an AI-powered "Virtual Asset Integrated Analysis System"

Tracks 5-year transaction history using exchange data + on-chain + overseas via CARF

Handles up to 8 billion tx/year#southkorea#btc

— Dr.K (@Korean_Mansuru) March 11, 2026

A contractor will be selected by the end of March 2026, with the formal design phase starting in April.

The agency plans to run a pilot version of the software in November 2026, aiming for a full-scale launch by December.

The NTS stated regarding the project's launch,

“It is expected to serve our goal of collecting individuals’ virtual asset transaction data starting in 2027.”

What Are The Specific Tax Rates For Investors

Investors will need to keep a close eye on their gains, as the threshold for taxation is relatively low.

Any annual profit exceeding 2.5 million won (roughly $1,700) will be hit with a combined tax rate of 22%.

This total is split between a 20% national income tax and a 2% local surcharge.

This rule applies to a wide range of digital activities, as lawmakers are currently debating how to integrate airdrops and staking rewards into the final calculations.

🚨BREAKING: South Korea's NTS launches AI tracking for 2027 crypto tax! No more hiding! NTS is spending $2M on a system to track every move. AI will flag any suspicious patterns or "hidden" gains. Over 2.5M KRW profit? That's a 22% cut for the taxman. 🤔💸 pic.twitter.com/ii65JTOH8c

— Ghost Node (@Ghost_Vault666) March 12, 2026

Those who fail to report their earnings accurately could face heavy surcharges and aggressive audits once the AI system identifies the discrepancies.

Who Will Access Your Private Transaction Data

The NTS does not plan to keep its findings in a vacuum.

To create a watertight net around the digital asset market, the agency intends to share lists of suspected tax offenders and transaction analysis with other major government bodies.

This inter-agency cooperation includes the Korea Customs Service, the Bank of Korea, and the Ministry of Data and Statistics.

By pooling resources, the government hopes to identify delinquent taxpayers who might be using crypto to hide assets from other local taxes or even unpaid traffic fines.

Is Political Pressure Still Affecting The Rollout

Despite the clear technical preparations, the road to January 2027 has been paved with political hesitation.

Since the tax law was first passed in 2020, it has become a "political hot potato," often delayed by politicians eager to secure the votes of younger, tech-savvy citizens.

🇺🇸 | 🇰🇷

🇰🇷 South Korea Crypto Tax | Jan 2027 Implementation Planned, But 4th Delay Possible

🧠 Takeaway
Crypto tax implementation date set for Jan 1, 2027 → But enforcement regulations still incomplete, 4th delay possibility raised.

📌 FACT
• Implementation date: January 1,… pic.twitter.com/cfRg6LVtDE

— CoinEasy | Web3 Simplified (@Coiniseasy) February 20, 2026

Both sides of the National Assembly have previously used tax delays as a bargaining chip during election cycles.

However, the current investment of $2 million into a custom-built AI platform suggests that the government is finally moving past the debate phase and into the enforcement phase.

data.title: AI Agent needs Crypto, not Crypto needs AI
data.description:
Author: Scarlett Zhang; Source: X, @xiaohangzhang7

I feel more and more strongly that the crypto community is desperately trying to be seen by the AI ​​community.

In the past six months, you'll find that the entire crypto community has been trying very hard to get close to AI. Talking about AI, switching to AI, holding AI events, making AI demos, revising AI narratives, they want every project to find a way to prove that it has some connection with AI.

It feels a lot like: a child desperately trying to squeeze onto an adult's table.

But what about the other side?

Many people truly working in AI have a very nuanced attitude towards crypto. It's not about openly criticizing or outright rejecting it, but rather a very dignified sense of detachment: "We're not opposed to blockchain, but we don't want to be too deeply involved with crypto for now." "Technically, it's very interesting, but our clients and investors will mind." In other words: "You're a decent person, but I don't want to be involved in your circle." There is indeed a subtle hierarchy of disdain between these two circles, and it's not without reason. Many AI builders believe that AI is a genuine productivity revolution, technological progress, something that changes the way we work, the form of products, and the way information flows. And crypto? In their eyes, it's more like an overly financialized, narrative-driven industry that's always looking for the next story to prove its importance, and incidentally, a convenient way to issue tokens and fleece investors. So when the crypto community suddenly started talking about AI on a large scale, many people in the AI ​​community's first reaction was: Are you guys seriously making products, or are you just jumping on the bandwagon again? Frankly, I completely understand this reaction. Because over the past few years, crypto has indeed become too adept at packaging the "next wave." DeFi/NFT/GameFi/SocialFi/DePIN/Inscription, now it's AI x Crypto. In each wave, someone sews the latest buzzword onto themselves and tells you the future is here. Over time, the outside world has developed a difficult-to-reverse impression of crypto: You're always talking about the future, but it always makes people wonder if you're actually creating value or just creating an atmosphere. This is why many people in the AI ​​community today naturally feel they are in a superior position. They believe: AI is solving real problems; Crypto is still searching for new legitimacy. This bias is very real. This hierarchy of contempt certainly exists. But the more I think about it lately, the more interesting it becomes isn't why Crypto is so eager to get closer to AI, but rather another, more counterintuitive question: Will it ultimately be AI that truly needs the other? More precisely: It's not Crypto that needs AI. It's AI Agents that need Crypto. This isn't a question of "AI being smarter," but rather "Can AI manage money?" I'm increasingly convinced of this because many agent demos ultimately get stuck at the same point. Lately, everyone has probably seen quite a few demos: those that can write code, those that can call tools, those that can automatically browse web pages, those that can execute multi-step tasks, and even those that can handle transactions, payments, and automated workflows. At first glance, they seem very cool. But after seeing so many, I'm increasingly concerned about one question: Does it merely "know how," or can it actually "do"? Because the difference between "knowing how" and "doing" isn't just a matter of minor product details. The difference lies in: **authority, funding, responsibility, and boundaries.** Having an agent compile a report for you and having an agent complete a real transaction are completely different issues. If the former is wrong, you might at most think it's a bit stupid. If the latter is wrong, your money is gone. Therefore, I increasingly feel that AI demos are most likely to create an illusion: **everything seems almost perfect.** But what's truly not perfect is often the most difficult layer. That is: the **execution layer**.

If an AI agent truly starts doing things for you, it will soon need to buy APIs, rent computing power, call paid services, execute transactions, manage budgets, transfer assets, and complete payments between different systems.

In other words, it doesn't just need to "understand your intentions." It needs to start participating in economic activities. And once it enters this layer, the problem changes.

Traditional finance can embrace automation, but it wasn't designed for an "Agent world." Many readers might be wondering, "Traditional finance can do these things too." I've certainly considered that, and frankly, traditional finance is indeed more mature than crypto in many dimensions. Risk control, auditing, access management, chains of responsibility, and traceability—traditional finance is stronger in these areas today. Therefore, the real point of this article isn't that crypto is better than traditional finance, nor that AI agents are completely ineffective without crypto. If it's just an internal enterprise agent or a platform agent, many things can certainly still be done using bank APIs, enterprise payment systems, virtual cards, approval workflows, sub-account systems, platform credits, and centralized escrow accounts. These all work, and are likely to remain mainstream in the short term. However, the problem lies in the fact that these systems are essentially built on the same premise: **The Agent is not a native executor. It is merely an automated appendage of a user, company, or platform.** This works fine in many scenarios. But as agents become more autonomous, more cross-platform, more cross-border, and more reliant on native resource and fund access across different systems, traditional systems will become increasingly awkward. Therefore, the real issue isn't whether traditional finance can handle it, but whether it's the most natural, scalable, and natively agent-compatible structure. These are actually two completely different issues. The key to AI Agents isn't whether they are "legal entities," but rather that they are increasingly resembling "execution units." At this point, you might think, "But agents aren't third-class entities either. They're not people, nor are they companies; they're just software agents." That's true. Strictly speaking, an AI agent may not necessarily become an independent legal entity. Most of the time, it's more likely to be an agent for users, businesses, or platforms. Even so, it will increasingly resemble an execution unit that can be assigned **budgets, permissions, tasks, and boundaries**. This is the key point. The reason this problem hasn't fully surfaced today is because there aren't enough agents yet; many things are still in the stage of "humans watching it perform its tasks." But if in the future there are truly large-scale agents: helping you with transactions, helping you with procurement, helping you with operations, helping you manage budgets, helping you automatically allocate resources between systems. You'll encounter a very awkward problem: how exactly should these things be granted permissions? Whose account does it belong to? Whose payment authorization is it linked to? How much money can it spend? Who is responsible if it exceeds its authority? How is the underlying settlement handled when it calls services globally? Traditional finance can handle this. But it will become increasingly awkward. Because it was never designed based on the premise that "software execution units will participate in economic activities on a large scale."

Once the protagonist is changed to an Agent, crypto's previously abstract concepts begin to become concrete

Many people who read crypto in the past felt that it was always talking about very vague terms:

  • Programmable funds

  • permissionless

  • global settlement

  • trustless execution

Many times it does sound like a bunch of nonsense.

But if you change the protagonist to an AI agent, these concepts suddenly become less abstract.

Because what the agent really needs might just be these things:

  • It needs a natively callable form of funds.

  • It needs an execution identity that doesn't have to first become a "company account".

  • It requires budgets and permissions that can be programmatically constrained. It needs to achieve low-friction settlement globally. It needs to establish a native connection between invocation behavior and asset behavior. At this point, your perspective on a wallet will be completely different. A wallet is not just a "place to store coins." What is it more like? More like an execution container with permission boundaries.

    It holds more than just assets. It can also set rules: What is allowed? How much is allowed to spend? Which actions can be executed automatically? What thresholds require manual confirmation? Which scenarios are read-only, which are write-only? Which policies are effective on-chain, and which must be stopped? From this perspective, the relationship between AI and wallet is very interesting: AI is responsible for understanding, Wallet is responsible for constraints, and Agent is responsible for actions. This is what a complete system looks like. The real irony is: AI raises the question of trust, and crypto is precisely what it lacks most. If I were to take the side of an opponent, I would say: You just said that AI's real problem is trust, so why are you pointing to crypto as the answer? This criticism is reasonable. After all, in the eyes of most ordinary people, crypto is not a "naturally trustworthy" system. Private key management is complex, on-chain transactions are irreversible, phishing and signature theft are rampant, contracts are risky, liability boundaries are often blurred, and there may not be anyone to cover for you after something goes wrong. Therefore, what I really want to express is not that crypto has solved the problem of trust. On the contrary. My judgment is that AI will force crypto to answer "trust" directly. In the past, crypto could remain at the level of "can be transferred, can be used, and can run." But if it truly wants to become the execution layer of an AI agent, it must learn the most difficult lesson: Permission Model, Security Boundary, Attribution of Responsibility, Risk Control System, Recoverability, and Human-Machine Collaboration Confirmation Mechanism. In other words, AI won't automatically create crypto. Instead, AI will force out all the most ambiguous, lazy, and narrative-driven aspects of crypto in the past. So I'm not saying crypto is already the answer. I'm saying: **If agent-native execution infrastructure truly exists in the future, it will most likely resemble crypto more than today's traditional account systems.** Therefore, the question may never have been "How can crypto leverage AI to revive its popularity?"
